content:
{
  "title": "Reverse-Context Prompting for Enhanced Code Generation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current code generation prompts for LLMs predominantly present task context followed by the code to be produced, potentially biasing models to surface-level pattern matching. This can limit models' ability to generalize to unseen code structures or tasks.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLMs' code generation is essential for automating software development and assisting programmers. As models become more capable, challenging them with more abstract or reversed prompt structures may foster deeper reasoning and compositional capabilities, which is timely given the increasing complexity of code tasks being automated.",
  "method": "We propose 'reverse-context prompting,' wherein the LLM is first shown a target code snippet and subsequently prompted to generate the context or specification that would produce it. During test time, we then reverse this process: we supply the model with such generated (or manually written) specifications, leveraging the model's strengthened bidirectional mapping between code and context. This approach encourages the model to internalize deeper correspondences between problem and solution, rather than shallow pattern completion. Training and inference pipelines are adapted to support this prompt reversal mechanism.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on HumanEval and MBPP, comparing against standard code-generation prompting and chain-of-thought baselines. Metrics will include pass@k, BLEU, and specification-code consistency. Ablations will assess the impact of pre-exposing models to reverse-context prompts versus standard prompts."
}
